planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; shooting star is a spring ephemeral that dies back completely by early summer — mark planting location to avoid disturbing the dormant roots during summer dormancy.

directions: Plant in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade — shooting star is one of the most spectacular native spring wildflowers, producing umbels of 3–20 nodding flowers whose swept-back petals (white to lavender to deep magenta) and prominent yellow anther cone pointing downward create an unmistakable shooting-star silhouette. The flowers are buzz-pollinated exclusively by bumblebees, which grip the anther cone and vibrate at a specific frequency to release the pollen. A spring ephemeral of open woodland, prairies, and moist slopes, shooting star goes completely dormant by early summer and must be marked to avoid disturbing the underground corms. It grows slowly and persists for decades in appropriate conditions. Requires good drainage despite needing moisture in spring — summer-wet conditions cause rot. Outstanding for naturalizing in moist, open woodland gardens with hostas, ferns, and other spring wildflowers.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants or divisions in fall in very well-drained, strongly acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), sandy or rocky, dry, poor soil in full sun to partial shade; birdfoot violet will not thrive in clay, wet, or fertile soils — it requires the conditions of the sandy pine barrens and dry rocky glades in which it is native.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, strongly acidic, sandy or rocky, dry, poor soil in full sun to partial shade — birdfoot violet is one of the most beautiful of all native violets, with the largest and most vividly colored flowers of the eastern species — 1 inch lavender to pale violet or bicolored (the classic bicolor form has dark upper petals and pale lower petals with an orange eye). The deeply divided, multi-lobed leaves closely resemble a bird's foot, giving the plant its common name. Unlike most violets that thrive in moist, rich soil, birdfoot violet is strictly a plant of dry, poor, acidic, sandy, or rocky soils — pine barrens, sandstone outcrops, and dry, open glades where soil fertility is minimal. It will languish and die in clay or rich soil. An outstanding and distinctive native for sandy, acidic rock gardens and dry native plant gardens in zones 3–8; a specialist larval host for several native fritillary butterflies.

planting.note: Direct sow seed in fall or divide clumps in early spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Canada violet is one of the tallest and most shade-tolerant native violets, producing flowers over a longer season than most violets.

directions: Sow seed in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade — Canada violet is one of the most garden-worthy and longest-blooming native violets, producing white, five-petaled flowers with a distinctive purple-veined lower petal and faint purple reverse on the upper petals from spring through early summer on stems 8–16 inches tall — significantly taller than most native violets. It grows naturally in moist, rich, shaded forests and mountain woodland throughout northern North America and is genuinely shade-adapted, performing well in deep woodland conditions where most violets cannot thrive. The heart-shaped leaves are attractive throughout the season. Canada violet spreads moderately by rhizome and self-sows to naturalize, forming groundcover colonies in appropriate conditions. All violet species are larval host plants for native fritillary butterflies, making them essential components of the native butterfly garden. An outstanding shade-garden violet for zones 3–8.

planting.note: Direct sow seed in fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; downy yellow violet is the primary yellow-flowered violet of eastern woodland understories and naturalizes readily by self-seeding in appropriate conditions.

directions: Sow seed in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade — downy yellow violet is the most common and widespread yellow-flowered native violet of eastern deciduous woodlands, producing bright yellow, purple-veined five-petaled flowers on hairy stems 6–14 inches tall in early to mid-spring, simultaneously with the emergence of the trout lilies and trilliums in the same woodland community. The broad, heart-shaped, softly hairy leaves (giving the common names 'downy' and 'hairy') are attractive throughout the growing season. It grows naturally in rich, moist, deciduous hardwood forest from Canada to the Deep South. Like all violets, downy yellow violet is a critical larval host plant for native fritillary butterfly caterpillars (Speyeria and Boloria species) — the presence of violets in a garden directly supports breeding populations of these spectacular native butterflies. Self-sows modestly to naturalize. Outstanding native for spring woodland wildflower gardens.

planting.note: Direct sow cold-stratified seed in fall in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, thin, rocky, cedar glade soil in full sun; Tennessee coneflower is adapted to the extreme conditions of limestone cedar glades and requires excellent drainage — it will rot in clay or wet soils.

directions: Sow cold-stratified seed in fall in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, thin rocky soil in full sun — Tennessee coneflower is a Federally Threatened native species known from only a handful of cedar glade sites in Davidson and Rutherford counties of central Tennessee, where it grows in the extremely shallow, rocky, alkaline soil over limestone bedrock that characterizes these rare ecosystems. It is distinguished from other coneflowers by its upward-reflexed (rather than drooping) ray petals, giving the flower a more open, daisy-like appearance. The species was removed from the federal endangered species list in 2011 after recovery efforts increased its wild populations — a conservation success story. It performs well in gardens as a drought-tolerant, heat-hardy perennial for well-drained, sunny, alkaline to neutral soils in zones 4–9. Growing this species in cultivation helps maintain genetic diversity and reduces collection pressure on wild populations. Purchase only nursery-propagated plants.
